titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Records of the Asian/Pacific/American Institute RG.21.35
titlestmt.author: Lesley Espenship, John Bence and Lindsay Dumas. Additional accessions processed by Megan Findling and Aleksandr Gelfand.

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-02-24 15:31:50 UTC.

change.item: Updated by Rachel Searcy to reflect accession 2016.029.
Updated by Craig Savino to include accretion from accession 2016.024.
Updated by Kate Fisher to reflect accession 2017.055.
Updated by Rachel Searcy to reflect accession 2018.031
Updated by Rachel Mahre, Anna Björnsson, and Natalia Medero to state some audiovisual materials have been digitized and are accessible to patrons.
Updated by Anna Björnsson McCormick to correctly identify Wilfredo Lam
Updated by Rachel Searcy to reflect 2022 accretion
Updated by Nicole Greenhouse to reflect additional administrative information and added archived websites
Updated by Rachel Mahre to further arrange and describe digitized audiovisual materials

did.abstract: The records of the Asian/Pacific/American (A/P/A) Institute, dated 1994-2015, document the activities of the Institute and reflect student events, faculty affairs and collaborations with the A/P/A studies program. The collection contains photographs, audiovisual recordings, memoranda, correspondence, student surveys, meeting and conference agendas, syllabi, proposals, posters, flyers, and brochures.

accessrestrict.head: Access Restrictions
accessrestrict.p: Administrative records and unpublished reports of New York University are closed for a period of 20 years from the date of their creation. Access to files spanning multiple years will be opened to researchers based on the date of the most recent materials. Board of Trustees records are closed for 35 years from the date of creation. Materials related to personnel, grievances, job and fellowship searches and applications, and all files that fall under the federal Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) are permanently restricted. Additional restrictions may apply to other materials in this collection. For questions regarding specific restrictions, please contact the University Archives.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: These materials transferred to the University Archives in four accessions in 2008, 2009, 2010, and 2011.
Additional accretions were received in 2016, 2017, 2018, and 2022; the accession number associated with these transfers are 2016.024, 2016.029, 2017.055, 2018.031, and 2022.082.

In 2014, http://www.yellowperilarchive.org/, http://people.stern.nyu.edu/jmy272/yp/static/, and http://people.stern.nyu.edu/jmy272/yp/ were selected by curators and captured through the use of Archive-It in 2014 as part of the Asian/Pacific/American Institute: Yellow Peril! Web Archive. Archive-It uses web crawling technology to capture websites at a scheduled time and displays only an archived copy, from the resulting WARC file, of the website. There is no accessioning information with these websites.

appraisal.head: Take Down Policy
appraisal.p: Archived websites are made accessible for purposes of education and research. NYU Libraries have given attribution to rights holders when possible; however, due to the nature of archival collections, we are not always able to identify this information.
If you hold the rights to materials in our archived websites that are unattributed, please let us know so that we may maintain accurate information about these materials.

If you are a rights holder and are concerned that you have found material on this website for which you have not granted permission (or is not covered by a copyright exception under US copyright laws), you may request the removal of the material from our site by submitting a notice, with the elements described below, to the special.collections@nyu.edu.

Please include the following in your notice: Identification of the material that you believe to be infringing and information sufficient to permit us to locate the material; your contact information, such as an address, telephone number, and email address; a statement that you are the owner, or authorized to act on behalf of the owner, of an exclusive right that is allegedly infringed and that you have a good-faith belief that use of the material in the manner complained of is not authorized by the copyright owner, its agent, or the law; a statement that the information in the notification is accurate and made under penalty of perjury; and your physical or electronic signature. Upon receiving a notice that includes the details listed above, we will remove the allegedly infringing material from public view while we assess the issues identified in your notice.

bioghist.head: Historical Note
bioghist.p: The Asian/Pacific/American (A/P/A) Institute was founded in 1996. The Institute is focused on community and intercultural studies within a broad, international and comparative framework.

The A/P/A Institute produces public events and symposia for the larger New York University and New York City communities. The Institute brings together scholars, community builders, and artists from New York City and beyond for events throughout the year, including conferences, seminars, book readings, film screenings, musical performances, and brown bag lunches. The Institute also co-sponsors events with student and community organizations, which complement the academic programs offered by the University and address the needs of the community at large.

The A/P/A has collaborated on several events and activities with the Office for African American, Latino, and Asian American Student Services (OASIS). OASIS was created in 1988 in response to the expressed needs of students of color for more University administrative support and commitment. Today the mission of OASIS continues to be carried out by the Center for Multicultural Education and Programs (CMEP) at NYU.

The A/P/A Institute also reached out to youth in New York City through the Asian Pacific American Youth Alliance (APAYA) from 1994-2008. This was a free program led by A/P/A and community volunteers throughout the school year that sought to support and encourage high school youth to become community leaders. These students learned about and taught others about the vibrant and diverse Asian Pacific American history, cultures, and experiences. Their weekly meetings throughout the school year culminated in a forum at NYU planned and led by APAYA students.

list.item: New York University. "Asian/Pacific/American Studies: The Institute." http://www.apa.nyu.edu
New York University. "Center for Multicultural Education and Programs: About Us." http://www.cmep.nyu.edu

phystech.head: Audiovisual Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Access to audiovisual materials in this collection is available through digitized access copies. Researchers may view an item's original container, but the media themselves are not available for playback because of preservation concerns. Materials that have already been digitized are noted in the collection's finding aid and can be requested in our reading room.

phystech.head: Born-Digital Access Policies and Procedures
phystech.p: Advance notice is required for the use of computer records. Original physical digital media is restricted. Born-digital materials have not been transferred and may not be available to researchers. Researchers may request access copies. To request that material be transferred, or if you are unsure if material has been transferred, please contact University Archives, special.collections@nyu.edu, 212-998-2596 with the collection name, collection number, and a description of the item(s) requested. A staff member will respond to you with further information.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Published citations should take the following form: Identification of item, date (if known); Records of the Asian/Pacific/American Institute; RG 21.35; box number; folder number or item identifier; New York University Archives, New York University Libraries.
To cite the archived website in this collection: Identification of item, date; Asian/Pacific/American Institute; RG 21.35; Wayback URL; New York University Archives, New York University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: This collection was processed by Lesley Espenship in 2008, John Bence and Lindsay Dumas in 2009, Megan Findling in 2012, and Aleksandr Gelfand in 2013.

In October 2016 Craig Savino added Series VIII: 2016 Accretion to reflect a new accretion of materials. Kate Fisher added Series IX : 2017 to reflect a second accretion of materials. These accretions were added to the resource as accessions and do not reflect the original Records of the Asian/Pacific/American Institute's series arrangement.

In October 2022 an accretion was rehoused in archival boxes and folders, and described on the series-level with a box-level inventory. Born-digital materials on physical carriers were identified, physically separated, and inventoried, but have not yet been forensically imaged, analyzed, or described. New York University Libraries follow professional standards and best practices when imaging, ingesting, and processing born-digital material in order to maintain the integrity and authenticity of the content.
In 2023, archived websites were added as Series XII.
In February 2026, subseries in Series VI, previously separated by format, were combined into one series. Recordings from the same event were listed together in nested description.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The records of the Asian/Pacific/American (A/P/A) Institute, dated 1994-2015, document the activities of the Institute from its founding in 1996 and reflect student events, faculty affairs and collaborations with the A/P/A studies program. The collection contains photographs, audiovisual recordings memoranda, correspondence, student surveys, meeting and conference agendas, syllabi, proposals, posters, flyers, and brochures.

userestrict.head: Use Restrictions
userestrict.p: Any rights (including copyright and related rights to publicity and privacy) held by the creator are maintained by New York University. Permission to publish or reproduce materials in this collection must be secured from New York University Archives, special.collections@nyu.edu, 212-998-2596.

appraisal.head: Appraisal
appraisal.p: In 2025, 4 duplicate VHS tapes, 2 blank miniDV tapes, and 8 blank audiocassettes were deaccessioned.

controlaccess.subject: Student activities.
Asian American college students.
controlaccess.corpname: New York University. Faculty of Arts and Science

accessrestrict.head: Access Restrictions
accessrestrict.p: Some personal information in this series has been restricted. Please contact the University Archives for details.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I contains information on the preparation and production of the Asians in America conference from 1994-1998, as well as other conferences of interest to the A/P/A community including Where is Home? Annual Conference for Chinese in the Americas, held in 1997 and Public Displays of Asianness, held in 1998. The records generally pertain to conference logistics, planning, and publicity.

Public Displays of Asianness Conference - Abstracts
Public Displays of Asianness Conference - The Cooler
Public Displays of Asianness Conference - Setup

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is divided into four subseries that relate to specific A/P/A staff members. Records are arranged alphabetically within the subseries.

did.unittitle: Subseries A: Sheelagh Cabalda, Office for African American, Latino and Asian American Student Services (OASIS)

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Some of the material within this subseries is restricted because it contains personal information. The physical folders within this collection that are restricted and the boxes have been marked.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subseries A contains records pertaining to Sheelagh Cabalda's work at the Office for African American, Latino and Asian American Student Services (OASIS) in collaboration with A/P/A. This subseries documents various conferences, workshops and events produced by OASIS, A/P/A and other institutions. These records provide an understanding of student activities and events related to A/P/A Institute.

Subseries B: Tomio Geron, Academic Program Coordinator Asian/Pacific/American Studies Program

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subseries B contains correspondence, memoranda, and reports related to the further development of the A/P/A studies academic program. This includes new course proposals, staff and faculty job descriptions, the development of a mission statement, departmental magazines, and the creation of an alumni association. These records reflect the planning involved in the ongoing development of the academic program.

did.unittitle: Asian Pacific American Studies: Major Development
Asian Pacific American Studies: Minor Program Proposal
Asian Pacific American Studies: Program Meeting
Asian Pacific American Studies: Retreat Agenda
Working Session: Colonialism, Diaspora, and the Americas
Working Session: Community Studies and Curriculum
Working Session: South Asian American Studies
Subseries C: Risa Morimoto, Administrator and Asian/Pacific/American Institute Coordinator

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subseries C includes general course information related to the A/P/A program in addition to various events sponsored by the department. This subseries contains syllabi, course evaluations, course proposals and event records.

did.unittitle: Asian Pacific American Archives and Media Center
Development Correspondence: Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation
Development Correspondence: Carnegie Corporation of New York
End of the Year Party Logistics
Opening of A/P/A Studies Program and Institute
Syllabus: Filming Asia America, Documenting Community

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Subseries D contains syllabi from classes given at A/P/A.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III consists of information regarding various events and activities produced and sponsored by the A/P/A Institute. These records are primarily comprised of outreach materials, such as posters, brochures, and flyers. The majority of these events are art-related, such as gallery exhibitions, author reading series, and film screenings. Events also include lectures and discussions regarding various issues relevant to the A/P/A student community.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is divided into three subseries: a series of general materials organized alphabetically, a series of posters, and a series of general materials organized chronologically. The material in Subseries 3 was received at a later date, and its chronological organization has been retained.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV includes photographs of various events, students, faculty, and staff of the A/P/A Institute and the A/P/A studies program.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series V contains material on the Asian Pacific American Youth Alliance (APAYA), including program activities, surveys, and material pertaining to planning and logistics.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Since this subseries contains the records of a youth program, some of the material is restricted because it has recent students' personal and contact information. Folders containing such material have been marked.

did.unittitle: Comic Strip - Saturday Training "I am from..."

"Has APAYA made an impact on you? If at all?"
10th Annual Forum on A/P/A Youth Culture

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series VI contains recordings of lectures, symposia, and performances sponsored by the Institute. Events were often captured in multiple formats and camera angles. Titles for conference panels are unofficial summaries of the discussion topics.

dao.xlink:title: Panel on Asian American hip-hop culture

did.unittitle: Presentations -- Susaina Maira, Key Kool, Sayu Bhowani, David Parker

dao.xlink:title: Presentations -- Susaina Maira, Key Kool, Sayu Bhowani, David Parker

daodesc.p: Presentations -- Susaina Maira, Key Kool, Sayu Bhowani, David Parker

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Presentations include: Susaina Maira on immigration and labor, racist violence, and cultural appropriation; Key Kool on personal hip-hop experience; Sayu Bhowani on immigrant youth and parenthood; David Parker on his experience in China and the UK.

phystech.head: Physical Characteristics and Technical Requirements
phystech.p: Duration: 94 min; Preservation Note: Side 1 was recorded at .9375 ips, captured at 1 7/8 ips and then digitally stretched. Side 2 of the tape is blank.

did.unittitle: Youth Identity Forum -- Exploring the Unseen and Unspoken

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series VII contains a large A/P/A banner.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Yellow Peril! project was a collaborative blog and website with intention to preserve and recontextualize artifacts from the history of anti-Asian fear in the United States, Europe, and other white dominated cultures. The website was maintained by John Kuo Wei Tchen and Mark Tseng Putterman. It contains Tchen's curriculum for Yellow Peril class; images of digitized artifacts that perpetuate anti-Asian sentiment in Fales and Tamiment collections; and commentaries on Islamophobia, transnational adoption, and other stereotyping of Asians in media. The website was active in 2014-2015. Also includes a rendering of the homepage and a sample page of the website.

phystech.head: Physical Characteristics and Technical Requirements
phystech.p: Due to technical or privacy issues, archived websites may not be exact copies of the original website at the time of the web crawl. Certain file types will not be captured dependent on how they are embedded in the site. Other parts of websites that the crawler has difficulty capturing includes Javascript, streaming content, database-driven content, and highly interactive content. Full-Text searches of archived websites are available at https://archive-it.org/organizations/567.

appraisal.head: Appraisal
appraisal.p: Robots.txt (a piece of code designed to limit crawler activity within a website) was ignored.
